title: Keyword-only and positional-only parameters

overview.what:
Python lets you enforce HOW arguments must be passed using two special markers in the signature:

* (bare star) makes everything after it keyword-only — callers MUST name those arguments:

  def create_user(name, *, role='member', active=True):
      ...

  create_user('alice', role='admin')   # ok
  create_user('alice', 'admin')        # TypeError — role must be named

/ makes everything before it positional-only — callers CANNOT name those arguments:

  def build_path(base, endpoint, /):
      return base + endpoint

  build_path('/api', '/users')         # ok
  build_path(base='/api', endpoint='/users')  # TypeError

Both can appear together: def f(pos_only, /, normal, *, kw_only). / requires Python 3.8+.

Think of * as a 'name required from here on' wall, and / as a 'no naming allowed before this' wall.
overview.why: Keyword-only parameters make call sites self-documenting (create_user('alice', role='admin') is clearer than create_user('alice', 'admin')) and prevent callers from passing booleans in the wrong order. Positional-only parameters let library authors rename internal parameters without breaking callers.

why_learning_this: Writing functions where boolean flags must be named (e.g. send_email(..., *, dry_run=False)) to prevent silent argument-order bugs
Reading Python 3.8+ library APIs that use / in their signatures

common_mistakes.title: Passing a keyword-only argument positionally
common_mistakes.explanation: If a parameter follows * in the signature, you must name it. Passing it positionally raises TypeError.

common_mistakes.title: Naming a positional-only argument
common_mistakes.explanation: If a parameter precedes / in the signature, you cannot pass it by name. Doing so raises TypeError.

common_mistakes.title: Confusing * (keyword-only marker) with *args
common_mistakes.explanation: A bare * in the parameter list is just a separator — it collects nothing. *args is a named collector for extra positional arguments. They look similar but behave completely differently.

recall_questions.q: What does a bare * in a function signature enforce?
recall_questions.answer: All parameters after * must be passed as keyword arguments — they cannot be passed positionally.

recall_questions.q: What does / in a function signature enforce?
recall_questions.answer: All parameters before / must be passed positionally — they cannot be passed by name.

recall_questions.q: What practical benefit does using * to require keyword arguments provide?
recall_questions.answer: It makes call sites self-documenting and prevents bugs from passing boolean flags in the wrong order.

practice_tasks.title: Enforce named boolean flags
practice_tasks.prompt: Write send_notification(user_id, *, dry_run=False, urgent=False) that prints what it would do. Add a bare * so callers must name dry_run and urgent. Show one valid call and one call that would raise TypeError (comment it out with an explanation).
practice_tasks.solution:
def send_notification(user_id, *, dry_run=False, urgent=False):
    prefix = '[DRY RUN] ' if dry_run else ''
    urgency = 'URGENT ' if urgent else ''
    print(f'{prefix}{urgency}notify user {user_id}')

send_notification(42, dry_run=True, urgent=False)
send_notification(99, urgent=True)
# send_notification(42, True)  # TypeError: takes 1 positional argument

code_walkthrough.code:
def resize(width, height, /, *, quality='high'):
    print(f'{width}x{height} at {quality}')

resize(1920, 1080)
resize(1280, 720, quality='low')
code_walkthrough.focus: width and height are positional-only (before /); quality is keyword-only (after *) — the call site must name quality but cannot name width or height.

challenge.title: Safe cache setter
challenge.prompt: Write set_cache(key, value, /, *, ttl=60, overwrite=False) where key and value are positional-only (internal names may change) and ttl and overwrite are keyword-only flags. Print a summary line for each call. Demonstrate three calls: default, custom ttl, and overwrite=True.
challenge.solution:
def set_cache(key, value, /, *, ttl=60, overwrite=False):
    action = 'overwrite' if overwrite else 'set'
    print(f'{action} cache[{key!r}] = {value!r} (ttl={ttl}s)')

set_cache('session:abc', {'user_id': 1})
set_cache('rate:limit', 100, ttl=300)
set_cache('config:theme', 'dark', ttl=3600, overwrite=True)
